ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for braised/marinated peanuts; closest match is the general peanut food product class. Braising preparation and soy-sauce marinade are not captured in the ontology term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers raw/processed peanuts as a commodity. No specific FoodEx2 term exists for braised/marinated peanut preparations; this code is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised peanut dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of marinated braised peanuts is approximately 50 g, equivalent to a small side dish or starter portion as commonly served in Teochew and Cantonese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Peanuts, all types, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #16087; adapted for braised preparation with soy sauce and spices using Singapore HPB nutrient adjustment guidance
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base peanut macros and micronutrients sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (peanuts, all types, raw, FDC ID 174263). Sodium value elevated to 450 mg/100 g to reflect soy-sauce braising liquid absorption, consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on braised legume preparations. Input record values cross-checked against USDA data and retained where plausible. Saturated fat estimated at 3.4 g/100 g based on USDA fatty acid profile for raw peanuts (approximately 13.7% of total fat). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for the braised preparation is not directly available in an approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; peanuts entry (GI = 14, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 14 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain boiled/roasted peanuts (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Braised peanuts with soy sauce and spices are not independently tested; the braising process does not materially alter the glycemic response of peanuts given their dominant fat, protein, and fibre matrix. GL per 50 g serving calculated as: (14 × 12.5 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 50 g serving) / 100 / 100 = 0.875, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg/100 g) from soy-sauce braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 30 g per occasion and avoid consuming the residual braising liquid, as sodium intake from this dish can contribute significantly to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 14) due to high fat, protein, and dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Marinated braised peanuts have a very low glycemic index and glycemic load, making them a suitable snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, caloric density from fat (24.8 g/100 g) warrants portion control to avoid excess energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial unsaturated fat profile from peanuts offset by elevated sodium from braising
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanuts provide predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats which are cardioprotective, but the high sodium content (450 mg/100 g) from soy-sauce braising is a concern for cardiovascular disease management; consumption should be limited to small portions and low-sodium soy sauce substitutions are advisable where possible.

peanut_allergy.key_factor: Primary ingredient is peanuts — a major allergen
peanut_allergy.guidance: This dish must be strictly avoided by individuals with a peanut allergy, as peanuts are the sole primary ingredient and cross-contamination risk is high in shared kitchen environments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marinated Braised Peanuts (卤花生 / 鹵水花生, Lǔ Huāshēng) are a classic Chinese cold appetizer and small dish (Xiao Chi) deeply embedded in Teochew and Cantonese culinary tradition. Raw, shelled peanuts are slow-simmered for an extended period in a fragrant master stock (lu shui) infused with light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, star anise, cinnamon, ginger, and optionally licorice root or dried tangerine peel. The prolonged braising process renders the peanuts exceptionally soft and tender while deeply infusing them with the savory, aromatic, and subtly sweet flavors of the spice-laden stock. The finished peanuts are typically served chilled or at room temperature as a complimentary starter, a congee accompaniment, or a casual beer snack. Nutritionally, braised peanuts are energy-dense (approximately 320 kcal/100 g) with a favorable macronutrient profile: 14.5 g protein, 24.8 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 12.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Dietary fibre is notable at 4.5 g/100 g. The glycemic index is very low (GI 14), making this dish suitable for blood sugar management. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (450 mg/100 g) from the soy-based braising liquid, which limits suitability for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal. It is a major allergen food due to peanuts.
common_user_queries: Are braised peanuts good for diabetics?
Can I eat braised peanuts on a low-sodium diet?

confidence_reason: Base peanut macronutrient values are well-supported by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw peanuts. Sodium value is adapted upward to reflect soy-sauce braising absorption and is consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on braised legume preparations. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA fatty acid profile. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain peanuts and applied as an analogous estimate for the braised preparation. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific braised preparation, hence medium confidence.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the braised peanut preparation is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. Raw peanut fatty acid data from USDA (FDC 174263) could be used as a proxy but has not been applied here to avoid overstating precision for a processed preparation. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for braised peanuts is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore for this specific preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: peanuts and plant-based braising ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: peanuts are a plant food and contain no cholesterol; naturally zero. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0: peanuts contain negligible or no retinol/RAE vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, or vitamin B12; values consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for peanuts. 6. Saturated fat of 3.4 g/100 g is estimated from USDA fatty acid profile for raw peanuts (approximately 13.7% of total fat × 24.8 g fat). 7. The input record cited 'Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences' as a source; this organization is not on the approved source list for Project Veritas v2 and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and University of Sydney GI Database as approved sources. 8. Duplicate portion entry in input record (two identical 50 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 50 g serving.
